name: Finn mac Cumhaill
description: Finn mac Cumhaill, also known as Fionn mac Cumhaill, is a prominent hero in Irish mythology, particularly within the Fenian Cycle. He is portrayed as a wise, just, and courageous leader of the Fianna, a band of elite warriors sworn to protect the High King of Ireland. Finn's character is multifaceted, embodying not only martial prowess but also possessing extraordinary wisdom and prophetic abilities. He gained his wisdom by accidentally tasting the Salmon of Knowledge. Finn's adventures are filled with encounters with mythical creatures, battles against formidable foes, and the exploration of themes such as leadership, loyalty, and the balance between the human and supernatural realms. His stories have been passed down through oral tradition and later written in texts like the *Acallam na Senórac* (Colloquy of the Ancients) solidifying his place as one of Ireland's most enduring heroes. His legacy extends beyond Ireland influencing literature and folklore across the Celtic diaspora. He is a central figure in understanding early Irish heroic ideals.
subtitle: Wise Leader of the Fianna

keyMyths.title: The Salmon of Knowledge
keyMyths.description: Finn's acquisition of his wisdom is a pivotal moment. He was instructed to cook the Salmon of Knowledge for his mentor, Finegas. Finegas had been trying to catch the salmon for years, as whoever ate it would gain all the world's knowledge. While cooking, Finn burned his thumb and instinctively put it in his mouth to cool it. This act gave him the salmon's wisdom instead of Finegas. From that moment on, whenever Finn needed insight, he would suck his thumb.
keyMyths.source: Rolleston, T. W. *Myths and Legends of the Celtic Race*. George G. Harrap & Company, 1911.

keyMyths.title: The Building of the Causeway
keyMyths.description: One tale attributes the creation of the Giant's Causeway to Finn. The legend states that Finn built the causeway to cross the sea to Scotland to fight the giant Benandonner. Variations of the story describe Finn either defeating Benandonner or cleverly tricking him by disguising himself as a baby, leading Benandonner to believe Finn's father must be even larger and more formidable, causing him to flee.
keyMyths.source: Gregory, Augusta. *Gods and Fighting Men*. John Murray, 1904.

keyMyths.title: The Pursuit of Diarmuid and Gráinne
keyMyths.description: This tragic love triangle involves Finn, Diarmuid Ua Duibhne, and Gráinne, the daughter of King Cormac mac Airt. Finn, despite his age, was betrothed to Gráinne. However, Gráinne fell in love with Diarmuid and eloped with him. Finn pursued them relentlessly, leading to a series of adventures and hardships for the fleeing lovers. The story ends tragically with Diarmuid's death and Gráinne's eventual acceptance of Finn.
keyMyths.source: Cross, Tom Peete, and Clark Harris Slover. *Ancient Irish Tales*. Barnes & Noble Books, 1969.

sources.author: Ellis Davidson, H.R.
